hook: You already know that computers speak in 0s and 1s — but have you ever wondered how those simple bits combine into the logical decisions powering every app, circuit, and AI you use? So far, you've worked with logic and sets in the abstract; this unit reveals how those ideas are physically built into hardware and computationally encoded into machines. By the end, you'll understand not just *what* computers compute, but *how* and *what they fundamentally cannot* compute.
concept: This unit connects the algebra of logic to the theory of computation: starting from the simplest logic gates and building all the way up to the theoretical limits of what any machine can do — a journey captured by the phrase *formal language theory and the Chomsky–Turing hierarchy*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to represent logical decisions as Boolean functions and translate them directly into the gate-level circuits that form the building blocks of all digital hardware.
Master the Karnaugh map technique to systematically simplify Boolean expressions, reducing circuit complexity and making designs faster and cheaper to implement.

Apply the finite state machine model to design systems that process sequences of inputs and transition between states — the same framework behind traffic lights, vending machines, and network protocols.

You will learn to characterize the class of languages that finite automata can recognize, and use regular expressions — the same syntax used in programming and text search — to describe these patterns precisely.

Explore the theoretical model of computation that defines the ultimate boundary of what algorithms can solve, and encounter the profound idea that some well-posed problems are provably unsolvable by any computer.
